serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Australian lobster is approximately 150g of cooked lobster meat, equivalent to roughly half a medium-sized lobster.

hypertension.guidance: Australian lobster contains 535mg sodium per 100g, placing it in the borderline-high range for hypertensive individuals; consume in portions no larger than 100g, request steamed or boiled preparation without added salt, and avoid high-sodium sauces such as garlic butter or black bean sauce to keep total meal sodium within the recommended 600mg per meal limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar with high protein at 20.91g per 100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Australian lobster is an excellent protein source with zero carbohydrate and zero sugar, producing no glycaemic response; it is well-suited for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes, provided it is prepared without starchy sauces or breading and served without high-carbohydrate accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol at 161mg per 100g offset by low saturated fat at 0.18g and beneficial omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Australian lobster has a favourable saturated fat profile at approximately 0.18g per 100g and provides omega-3 fatty acids that support cardiovascular health, but the dietary cholesterol content of 161mg per 100g warrants moderation for individuals with established cardiovascular disease or those following a strict dietary cholesterol restriction; limit to one serving of 100–150g and avoid butter-based preparations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids with negligible saturated fat and calcium at 106mg per 100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Australian lobster provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) and calcium at 106mg per 100g which supports bone health in rheumatoid arthritis patients on corticosteroids; avoid butter-based sauces and high-sodium preparations that may worsen joint inflammation and tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Australian Lobster (澳洲龙虾, Udang Karang Australia) is a premium crustacean sourced from Australian waters, widely served in Chinese restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia. It belongs to the spiny or rock lobster family (Palinuridae) and is prized for its firm, sweet, and slightly briny white meat. In Singapore's Chinese dining context, it is most commonly prepared steamed with garlic and glass noodles, baked with cheese, or wok-fried with ginger and spring onion. Per 100g of cooked lobster meat, it delivers 97 kcal, 20.91g of high-quality complete protein, only 0.95g of total fat (of which approximately 0.18g is saturated), and zero carbohydrate and sugar, making it one of the leanest and most protein-dense seafood options available. Sodium is elevated at 535mg per 100g due to the naturally saline marine environment of the crustacean, which is a consideration for hypertensive diners. Cholesterol is present at 161mg per 100g, which is moderate-to-high and relevant for cardiovascular disease management. Calcium at 106mg per 100g and magnesium at 48mg per 100g contribute to bone and metabolic health. Australian lobster is a shellfish allergen and is not suitable for individuals with crustacean allergies. It is gluten-free and dairy-free in its natural state. Clinically, it is an excellent choice for type 2 diabetes management due to its zero glycaemic impact, and it provides omega-3 fatty acids beneficial for joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis. It is typically served as a centrepiece dish at celebratory Chinese meals including Lunar New Year banquets and wedding dinners.

Can I eat lobster if I have high blood pressure?
